# Psalm 22: The Cry That Changes Everything

The raw anguish in verse 1 cuts straight to the bone. "My God, my God, why have You forsaken me?" This is not the cautious complaint of someone maintaining religious composure. This is abandonment. Silence in the face of desperation. The psalmist does not understand what is happening—he cries by day and by night, but heaven seems locked shut. Yet even in this devastation, something extraordinary happens: he insists on God's holiness and remembers that his fathers were delivered. He is holding fast to truth about God while his circumstances scream the opposite. This is the wrestling match we rarely see in prayer—not faith as the absence of doubt, but faith that refuses to release its grip on God even when He feels impossibly distant.

Then the psalmist's suffering becomes almost unbearably specific. His bones are out of joint. His heart liquefies within him. His strength evaporates. He is surrounded by enemies who mock and pierce him, dividing his clothes by casting lots. His tongue adheres to his mouth. The language is so precise, so bodily, that nearly every Christian throughout history has recognized in this poem the shadow of Christ's crucifixion—though the psalmist himself could not have known he was describing the Messiah's death. What matters is that this is a real person in real agony, and God's Word preserves that agony without explaining it away or rushing past it to find comfort.

The turning point comes in verse 22, sudden and decisive: "I will proclaim Your name to my brothers." The speaker moves from the depth of abandonment to the certainty of deliverance without any explanation of what changed. We are not told that the suffering ended or that circumstances improved. Instead, the psalmist simply shifts—because he has encountered God. What follows is not mere relief but explosive worship that radiates outward: his praise enters a great assembly, the poor eat and are satisfied, and people from the ends of the earth turn to the Lord. This is the heart of the psalm's mystery: the one who was forsaken becomes the voice through which nations come to know God's righteousness. His suffering is not senseless or abandoned after all—it is the pathway through which God's salvation reaches the world.

The distance between verses 1 and verses 22-31 teaches us something crucial about faith. We do not trust God because we understand His plan or because pain is proportional to our belief. We trust because we belong to Him, because He has demonstrated His power through history, and because—even in our deepest darkness—we belong to Him still. When you find yourself in the territory of verse 1, when God's silence presses against you with its full weight, remember that this prayer is Scripture. Your anguish is not foreign to God's Word. And the invitation before you is the same one the psalmist faced: Will you still call Him "My God"? Will you hold to His holiness even when you cannot see His hand? That simple refusal to let go may be the moment when your cry becomes the pathway through which others find Him.
